did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a biologically inspired QuadTree-based color segmentation algorithm to automatically detect and identify colors in dermoscopy images of melanoma. It employs machine learning classifiers, specifically Support Vector Machine (SVM), Artificial Neural Network (ANN), Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA), and Random Forest, to classify lesions as benign or malignant based on the extracted color features. The SVM classifier achieved the best performance with an AUC of 0.93.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development of a biologically inspired QuadTree-based color segmentation algorithm and its evaluation using four classifiers (SVM, ANN, LDA, Random Forest) on a dataset of 825 dermoscopy images. It reports specific results, including an AUC of 0.93 for the SVM classifier, and compares these findings against dermatologist assessments and pathological ground truth, which characterizes it as an original technical study rather than a review.

title: Biologically Inspired QuadTree Colour Detection in Dermoscopy Images of Melanoma
journal: IEEE Journal of Biomedical and Health Informatics
brief_summary: This study developed a biologically inspired QuadTree-based color segmentation algorithm to automatically detect and classify colors in dermoscopy images of melanoma, modeling expert dermatologist assessments. The method was evaluated on a dataset of 825 dermoscopy images (644 benign nevi and 181 malignant melanomas) obtained from NIH SBIR studies. Performance was assessed using four machine learning classifiers (SVM, ANN, LDA, Random Forest) with 10-fold cross-validation, comparing results against pathological ground truth and three dermatologists' assessments. The kernel Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifier achieved the highest performance with an Area Under the ROC Curve (AUC) of 0.93, outperforming the average dermatologist AUC of 0.82. The system successfully identified significantly more colors in melanomas than benign lesions and highlighted the diagnostic importance of specific colors like blue-grey, black, and pink. These results suggest the proposed method can assist in melanoma diagnosis by providing an accurate, transparent model of expert color perception.

lesion_types_studied.details: Malignant melanomas (n=181) were the primary target class, comprising 72 invasive melanomas and 109 melanomas in situ. These were obtained from NIH SBIR studies and confirmed by dermatopathologists.

Benign melanocytic nevi (n=644) served as the comparator class. They were confirmed to be benign by biopsy, dermoscopy, or monitoring with follow-up. The group included approximately equal numbers of dysplastic and non-dysplastic nevocellular nevi.

all_task_types.target: Binary diagnosis of melanoma (malignant) versus benign melanocytic nevus
all_task_types.details: The paper employs four machine learning classifiers (SVM, ANN, LDA, Random Forest) to classify dermoscopy images as benign or malignant based on extracted color features. The primary evaluation metric is the Area Under the ROC Curve (AUC), with the SVM classifier achieving an AUC of 0.93. The task is explicitly defined as a binary decision problem for melanoma recognition.

datasets_used.name: NIH SBIR Dermoscopy Study Dataset
datasets_used.description: A collection of dermoscopy images obtained from four clinics during 2007-2009 as part of NIH SBIR studies (R43 CA153927-01 and R44 CA101639-02A2). The images were acquired using Nikon DSC-W70 digital cameras and DermLite Fluid dermatoscopes. The dataset includes pathological ground truth for malignant lesions (confirmed by two dermatopathologists) and benign lesions (confirmed by biopsy, dermoscopy, or monitoring with follow-up).

use.training: Used for training the QuadTree-based color identification algorithm and evaluating classifier performance via 10-fold cross-validation.
use.testing: Used for testing classifier performance (SVM, ANN, LDA, Random Forest) and comparing against dermatologist assessments and pathological ground truth.
use.other: Used to establish ground truth for color detection experiments by three dermatologists (two from Royal Melbourne Hospital, one from University of Missouri).

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To estimate the cumulative KS distance between pixel intensities belonging to different quintiles and determine if samples are drawn from the same continuous population.
To determine the slope of the log of the average KS distance as a function of level, serving as a location descriptor.
To evaluate differences in the mean number of detected colours between malignant melanoma and benign groups, and to compare computer detection with dermatologist assessments.
To compute diagnostic performance metrics comparing the proposed colour detection method with dermatologists' assessments and clinical ground truth.

statistical_methods_used.method: Boxplot analysis (medians, 25th and 75th percentiles, standard deviations)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To visualize and compare the distribution of the number of detected colours in benign and malignant groups for both dermatologists and the computer algorithm.
To evaluate the performance of the machine learning classifiers (SVM, ANN, LDA, Random Forest) on the dataset.

hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no difference in the mean number of detected colours between malignant melanoma and benign melanocytic nevus groups.
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a difference in the mean number of detected colours between malignant melanoma and benign melanocytic nevus groups (specifically, malignant lesions have a higher number of colours).
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The independent samples t-test showed that the number of observed colours is significantly higher in the malignant melanoma group compared to the benign group (p-value < 0.001).

Experiment 1: Similarity in number of co.metric: Mean number of detected colours ± Standard Deviation
Experiment 1: Similarity in number of co.notes: Independent samples t-test showed significantly higher number of observed colours in the MM group compared to the Ben group (p-value < 0.001). The computer evaluation was similar to dermatologists in identifying the number of colours.

Experiment 2: Detection of four or more.notes: Computer accuracy in correctly identifying melanomas by the presence of 4-6 colours was significantly higher than that estimated by three dermatologists. The computer achieved better performance on all metrics.

Experiment 3: Detection of specific colo.metric: Sensitivity (SE), Specificity (SP), # Images (ratio of correctly identified to ground truth)
Experiment 3: Detection of specific colo.notes: Comparison between computer method and clinical ground truth (agreement between three dermatologists on 146 images). Light brown was present in all 124 Ben ground-truth images. The method performed best (88.4% to 100% agreement) in detecting dark brown, light brown, and blue-grey. Lower scores were obtained for white and red colours.

Experiment 4: Discriminatory colours and.metric: Odds Ratio (OR) for presence in Malignant vs Benign lesions
Experiment 4: Discriminatory colours and.notes: *Significant odds ratio (P < 0.05) in comparison with benign. Blue-grey and Red showed significant association with melanoma in the periphery. White showed significant association in the center. No discriminatory power for melanoma was found for light or dark brown.

LDA (Linear Discriminant Analysis).AUC: Not explicitly reported as a single value, but lower than SVM/RF

ANN (Artificial Neural Network).AUC: Not explicitly reported as a single value, but lower than SVM/RF

Classifier Performance (Binary Diagnosis.notes: The SVM classifier achieved the best results. The best two classifiers (SVM and Random Forest) performed better than the three dermatologists. Results are based on 10-fold cross-validation using 70% training and 30% testing split.

architecture_used.main_model: QuadTree-based color segmentation algorithm combined with four machine learning classifiers: Kernel Support Vector Machine (SVM), Artificial Neural Network (ANN), Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA), and Random Forest.

architecture_details.segmentation_method: Biologically inspired QuadTree segmentation modeled after expert dermatologist color perception. Uses median split color algorithm for quantization. Pre-processing includes color enhancement and contrast correction in the luminance plane of the CIE-Lab color space using top-hat and bottom-hat morphological transformations to correct non-uniform illumination. Lesions are divided into concentric quintiles for location analysis.
architecture_details.feature_extraction: Extracts 320 features including color shade, texture, and location information. Features include smoothness, skewness, uniformity, and entropy across four color planes.

training_details.normalization: Normalized feature vectors used for training and testing
training_details.hyperparameters: Classifier-specific parameters listed in architecture_details (e.g., SVM Gamma, ANN Size/decay, RF trees)

classification_or_output_head.output: Probability or decision score used to calculate AUC, Sensitivity, Specificity, and Accuracy

preprocessing_techniques: Color enhancement and contrast correction in the luminance plane of the CIE-Lab color space
Top-hat and bottom-hat morphological transformations to correct non-uniform illumination
Adding input dermoscopy image to top-hat filtered image to enhance bright features
Subtracting bottom-hat filtered image from input image to enhance dark features
Use of disk-shaped structuring element with radius of 18 for morphology transforms
Hair removal using directional morphological operations, filtering, and interpolation
Smoothing by a Gaussian low-pass filter
Lesion border detection using hybrid thresholding method (modification of Otsu threshold and adaptive histogram thresholding on optimized color channels)
Division of lesion into concentric quintiles for location analysis
Gray-scale resolution of 8 bit per pixel per color band
conclusions: The proposed biologically inspired QuadTree-based color segmentation algorithm provides an accurate model of expert dermatologist color assessment for melanoma detection.

The kernel Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifier achieved the best performance with an Area Under the ROC Curve (AUC) of 0.93, outperforming the average AUC of 0.82 achieved by three dermatologists.
The Random Forest classifier also performed well with an AUC of 0.87, while LDA and ANN classifiers performed lower than SVM and Random Forest.
The computer method identified significantly more colors in malignant melanomas compared to benign lesions, with a higher frequency of blue-grey, black, and pink colors in melanomas.
The method successfully delineated color locations by quintiles, showing a predilection for blue-grey and red in the periphery and a trend for white and black in the lesion center.

Blue-grey and red colors showed significant association with melanoma in the periphery, while white showed significant association in the center; no discriminatory power for melanoma was found for light or dark brown.

The proposed method achieved better diagnostic performance (sensitivity, specificity, and accuracy) than dermatologists when using the number of colors (4-6) as the sole feature for assessing malignancy.
The system has the advantage of transparency, as the detected colors and their locations mimic human color interpretation and can be immediately applied in the clinic.
The findings are confined to contact non-polarized dermoscopy images and may not be generalizable to images acquired by other means or different datasets without further study.
Agreement among dermatologists regarding color assessment was not high, suggesting that augmenting clinical assessments with computer algorithms could help reduce subjectivity.
Bias in color determination may have been present due to dermatologists' prior perception of lesion malignancy, which could influence color variable determination and diagnosis choice.

Future work includes applying the color palette and QuadTree clustering to larger, publicly available datasets and investigating the addition of crisp and fuzzy color descriptors to improve diagnosis.
important_remarks: The study is limited to contact non-polarized dermoscopy images acquired between 2007-2009; findings may not be generalizable to images acquired by other means or different datasets without further study.
There is a potential for bias in color determination because dermatologists determined the number of colors before making their diagnosis choice, which could influence color variable determination.
Agreement among the three dermatologists regarding color assessment was not high, indicating significant subjectivity in clinical color evaluation.
The algorithm showed poor performance in detecting red and white colors, with lower sensitivity and specificity compared to other colors like blue-grey and black.

The dataset consists of 825 images (644 benign, 181 malignant) from NIH SBIR studies; the malignant group included 72 invasive and 109 in situ melanomas, which may limit generalizability to broader populations.
The ground truth for specific color detection was established by requiring diagnostic agreement among three dermatologists on a subset of 146 images, which may introduce selection bias.
The study compares the proposed method against only three dermatologists; larger-scale clinical validation is needed to confirm the potential role in clinic diagnosis.
The QuadTree segmentation and color quantization were modeled after expert dermatologist assessments, which may propagate existing expert biases or limitations in color perception.
